title: The Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
September 29, 2019 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost in the liturgical calendar, falling within Ordinary Time (the long green season between Pentecost and Advent). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would include Esther 7:1-6, 9-10 and 9:20-22 (or Jeremiah 29:1, 4-7), Psalm 124, James 5:13-20, and Mark 9:38-50, focusing on themes of faithfulness, prayer, and Jesus's teachings on discipleship and welcoming the least among us. The lectionary had recently shifted into fall readings emphasizing practical Christian living and community accountability.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

September 2019 was still processing the aftermath of the 79th General Convention held in Austin, Texas (July 2018), which had made significant decisions on marriage equality and pastoral inclusion. The Episcopal Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Robert O'Neill (who had served since 2009), was navigating the post-convention landscape of increasingly inclusive policies. Saint John's Cathedral, as the mother church of the Diocese of Colorado, would have been engaged with diocesan synod preparations and ongoing conversations about the Church's prophetic voice on social justice issues.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical late September weather transitions, with cooling temperatures after a warm summer. The Colorado legislature was in recess between sessions, but the city was engaged in ongoing discussions about affordable housing and development pressures in Capitol Hill and surrounding downtown neighborhoods. The University of Colorado football season was underway, and the Denver Broncos were in the early weeks of their 2019 NFL season, generating significant local sports focus.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant national story was the impeachment inquiry into President Donald Trump, formally launched by House Speaker Nancy Pelosi on September 24, 2019, following the Ukraine whistleblower complaint about Trump's July phone call with President Zelensky. The 2020 Democratic primary race was intensifying with the third debate held on September 12, featuring Joe Biden, Elizabeth Warren, and Bernie Sanders. Climate activism was accelerating nationwide, with the Global Climate Strike on September 20, 2019, drawing hundreds of thousands of protesters inspired by Greta Thunberg's activism.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Kashmir conflict remained volatile following India's August revocation of Kashmir's special status, with ongoing tensions and violence through September. The Hong Kong pro-democracy protests were in their fourth month, with clashes between demonstrators and police intensifying around the time of this sermon. Hurricane Dorian had devastated the Bahamas earlier in the month (September 1-3) and was affecting the Carolinas, generating significant disaster relief discussions in American churches.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was experiencing deep political polarization with the impeachment inquiry dominating news cycles and social conversation. There was a growing sense of climate urgency particularly among younger Americans and religious communities beginning to engage more openly with creation care theology. The entertainment world was focused on fall television returns and upcoming awards season; the Denver Broncos' ongoing struggles provided local cultural disappointment, while the city maintained its identity as an outdoor recreation and progressive hub increasingly aware of its role in national political conversations.
